description: Deeper analysis of the interrelationships between physical culture and sport: social functions of physical education, civilisation-related health problems and sport's compensatory role, and differences between elite and mass sport.

questions.text: How do the concepts of physical education and sport differ?
questions.explanation: Physical education is an organised, pedagogically oriented process of physical development (primarily within the education system), while sport is a competitive, performance-oriented physical activity whose goal is to maximise performance.

options.text: There is no difference; they are the same concept
Physical education: a pedagogical process; sport: competitive, performance-oriented activity
Physical education applies only to adults
Sport is exclusively the activity of professional athletes

questions.text: Sport and regular physical activity are the primary means of addressing civilisation-related health problems (e.g., physical inactivity).
questions.explanation: True. Regular physical activity is the most effective means of compensating for cardiovascular, musculoskeletal, and metabolic problems caused by hypokinesia (a physically inactive lifestyle) — this is one of the most important social functions of sport and physical education.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What is the concept of physical culture and what does it encompass?
questions.explanation: Physical culture is that part of human culture directed towards the development, care, and maintenance of the body's performance capacity — encompassing physical education, sport, hygiene, and health-related lifestyle habits.

options.text: Exclusively the domain of competitive sport
The part of human culture directed at body development, care, and maintenance of performance capacity
Only the content of the school physical education subject
The statistical measurement of sports performance

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT typically considered a civilisation-related health problem?
questions.explanation: Physical overloading (overtraining) belongs to the domain of sports science, not civilisation-related health problems. Physical inactivity, obesity, postural problems, and cardiovascular diseases are typical civilisation-related problems associated with a sedentary lifestyle.

options.text: Physically inactive lifestyle (hypokinesia)
Overtraining resulting from physical overloading
Postural problems resulting from sedentary work
Obesity and metabolic syndrome

questions.text: What is the difference between elite sport and mass sport in terms of objectives?
questions.explanation: The primary goal of elite sport is to achieve maximum sports performance under competitive conditions; the goal of mass sport is health preservation, recreation, and social community-building — generally not competition-oriented.

options.text: There is no difference; both are competition-oriented
Elite sport: achieving maximum performance; mass sport: health, recreation, community-building
Mass sport is more intensive than elite sport
Elite sport is exclusively for young people

questions.text: The health effects of sport and physical education are exclusively physical in nature.
questions.explanation: False. Regular physical activity demonstrably improves mental health as well: it reduces the risk of depression and anxiety, improves self-esteem and cognitive functions — health must be understood as a complex (physical, mental, social) phenomenon.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What is hypokinesia and what are its consequences?
questions.explanation: Hypokinesia denotes a lack of physical activity, which is perpetuated by a sedentary lifestyle. Its consequences include cardiovascular disease, obesity, osteoporosis, muscle atrophy, reduced cognitive performance, and metabolic disorders.

options.text: Fatigue resulting from excessively intense physical activity
Physical inactivity — consequences: cardiovascular disease, obesity, osteoporosis, and metabolic disorders
A type of sports injury
A hyperkinetic form of sport

questions.text: Sport's educational function lies in developing character, cooperation, and the spirit of fair play.
questions.explanation: True. The educational value of sport has long been recognised: following rules, accepting victory and defeat, teamwork, and self-discipline are all values transmitted through sports participation.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: Why can recreational sport be considered a means of maintaining work capacity?
questions.explanation: Recreational sport provides rest and active recovery for those engaged in mental and physical work: it reduces stress, improves blood circulation, and the endorphin release caused by movement improves mood and concentration — thereby maintaining long-term work capacity.

options.text: Because every athlete can reach professional level through recreation
Through stress reduction, active recovery, and endorphin release, it maintains work capacity
Recreation is exclusively rest, not movement
Sport reduces mental performance due to physical fatigue

questions.text: The level of physical culture in a society is determined exclusively by the number of Olympic medals.
questions.explanation: False. The level of physical culture is based on a complex indicator: the prevalence of mass sport, the proportion of people living an active lifestyle, the accessibility of sports infrastructure, and health indicators all characterise it together — Olympic medals reflect only the level of elite sport.

options.text: True
False
